title: Why errors are unknown in TypeScript
instruction:
Unlike Java or C#, JavaScript can throw anything — strings, numbers, objects, not just Error instances. TypeScript reflects this by typing caught errors as `unknown`, forcing you to check before using them.

See how to safely handle a caught error.
successMessage: `error instanceof Error` is the standard narrowing check. After it, TypeScript knows `error` is an `Error` instance, making `.message` safe to access.

title: Custom error classes — specific error types
instruction:
Custom error classes let you distinguish between different failure modes. `instanceof` checks are cleaner than checking error messages (which can change).

Build a set of domain-specific errors.
expectedOutput: Validation failed on 'email': Invalid email format
successMessage: Custom errors form a hierarchy — `ValidationError instanceof AppError` is also true. TypeScript narrows to the most specific match, giving you access to the custom fields like `.field` and `.code`.

title: Error factories — consistent error creation
instruction:
Error factory functions standardize how errors are created across your codebase. Instead of `throw new Error(...)` scattered everywhere, you call `errors.badRequest(...)` and get a consistent structure.

Build an error factory for an API.
successMessage: Error factories produce consistent, structured errors. In production, these would be returned as JSON responses. The factory pattern centralizes error creation so every API error looks the same.

title: The Result type — no throwing
instruction:
Instead of throwing errors (which break control flow), some codebases use a `Result` type that wraps either a success value or an error. The caller must check which it is.

Implement a safe file parser using Result.
expectedOutput: Invalid: Age -5 is out of range
Invalid: Age 200 is out of range
successMessage: The `Result` type forces callers to handle both the success and failure cases before using the value. Unlike throwing, control flow is never broken — the caller decides what to do with the error.

title: Chaining Results — transforming safely
instruction:
When you have multiple operations that can each fail, you often need to chain them. A `map` function on Result lets you transform the success value without unwrapping.

Chain multiple validation steps.
successMessage: Chained Results short-circuit on the first failure — if `parseNumber` fails, `ensurePositive` and `ensureInteger` are never called. The error propagates through automatically.

title: try/catch with typed errors — a helper function
instruction:
Wrapping `try/catch` in a helper function lets you standardize error handling and remove repetitive error-narrowing code.

Build a `tryCatch` helper that always returns a Result.
successMessage: `tryCatch` converts the unpredictable try/catch pattern into a predictable Result. The error narrowing (`error instanceof Error ? error : new Error(String(error))`) happens once in the helper — not at every call site.

title: finally — always runs
instruction:
`finally` runs after `try` and `catch`, regardless of whether an error was thrown. It's used for cleanup: closing connections, releasing locks, logging.

Build a resource manager that always cleans up.
successMessage: `finally` ensured the connection was closed even when an error was thrown. Without `finally`, DB2's connection would leak. This pattern is critical for database connections, file handles, and network sockets.

title: Re-throwing errors — filter what you handle
instruction:
Sometimes you can only handle specific error types and need to pass others up the call stack. Re-throwing lets you handle what you can and propagate the rest.

Build error handling that only catches specific errors.
successMessage: Each catch branch handles what it understands. Unknown errors are logged (in production, they'd be re-thrown). `TimeoutError instanceof NetworkError` is also true — the order of `instanceof` checks matters.

title: Assertion functions — throw or guarantee
instruction:
An assertion function throws if a condition isn't met and otherwise tells TypeScript "after this call, the condition is definitely true". This is useful for validating function inputs.

Build input validators using assertion functions.
expectedOutput: Error: userId must not be null or undefined
Error: amount must be positive, got -10
successMessage: After `assertDefined(userId, ...)`, TypeScript narrows `userId` from `number | null` to `number`. Assertion functions are like type guards but they throw instead of returning boolean — they're used for input validation.

title: Error aggregation — collect all failures
instruction:
Sometimes you want to collect ALL errors from a validation, not just the first one. Return all errors in one pass so users can fix everything at once.

Build a form validator that collects all errors.
expectedOutput: - Username must be at least 3 characters
- Password must be at least 8 characters
successMessage: Collecting all errors (instead of throwing on the first) gives users a complete picture of what needs fixing. This is standard for form validation — every web form you've ever filled out works this way.

title: Error serialization — errors across boundaries
instruction:
Errors can't be directly serialized to JSON — you must extract the relevant fields. This is essential when sending errors over HTTP or storing them in logs.

Serialize and deserialize errors for an API response.
successMessage: `JSON.stringify(new Error(...))` produces `{}` — errors don't serialize automatically. `serializeError` extracts the meaningful fields. This pattern is used in every production logging system.

title: Typed error boundaries — catch what you expect
instruction:
A well-designed system only throws errors of known types. You can build error handlers that are typed to specific error hierarchies, giving you guaranteed access to custom fields.

Build a layered error handler for different application layers.
expectedOutput: DB Error (query: SELECT * FORM users): Syntax error
successMessage: The `AppLayerError` union guarantees `formatError` only receives known error types — and TypeScript ensures both cases are handled. Inside each branch, the custom fields (`query`, `statusCode`) are safely accessible.
